summary: Gruber dismantles iSuppli's widely-cited claims that Apple earns 55% profit margins on the iPhone, arguing their 'bill of materials' analysis is fundamentally misleading. He notes iSuppli published their cost estimate months before the iPhone shipped, then conveniently confirmed their own guess after teardown. Gruber points out that Apple's actual reported gross margins are 25-36%, far below iSuppli's claims, and that component costs ignore software development, build quality, packaging, shipping, and warranty expenses. He criticizes the press for uncritically parroting iSuppli's estimates as fact, with dozens of stories citing no other source. The $7 line item for iPhone OS X licensing perfectly encapsulates the absurdity of reducing a product to its physical parts.
key_points: iSuppli published iPhone cost estimates in January 2007, months before the device shipped, then claimed vindication after a July teardown — a suspiciously convenient confirmation of their own guesses
Apple's legally-required quarterly financial statements show gross margins of 25-36%, far below the 50%+ margins iSuppli consistently claims for individual products
Bill-of-materials analysis fundamentally ignores software development, build quality, packaging, shipping, and warranty costs — making the resulting 'margin' figures meaningless
Dozens of news outlets parroted iSuppli's estimates as fact without citing any corroborating source or questioning the methodology
The $7 line item for 'Operating System - OSX' reveals the absurdity of trying to reduce the iPhone's value to its physical components
money_quotes: In early July, they took some actual iPhones apart, and, surprise surprise, concluded they were pretty much spot-on back in January, with Apple earning margins of 55 percent.
Warwick shifts from citing the figures as an estimate in one sentence to stating them as fact in the next.
We do not know that iPhone margins are 55 percent; what we know is that iSuppli, a company with no relationship to Apple whatsoever, claims Apple's iPhone margins are 55 percent.
The total cost of a product's physical components is not the total cost to produce the finished product.
The most precious iPhone ingredient, for example, is its software.
Try calling Apple and telling them you'd like to license OS X for your upcoming new cell phone, for $7 per unit.
key_insight: A product's bill of materials tells you almost nothing about its actual profit margins, and the press's uncritical repetition of iSuppli's component-cost estimates as margin analysis is lazy reporting that fundamentally misunderstands how products are made and valued.
